goal_progress.config.title: A New Framework for Measuring Progress
goal_progress.config.abstract: Measuring progress in development is complex, and simple metrics often provide conflicting results. This explainer introduces a measure that compares a country's progress against other countries at a similar stage of development. The approach offers a contextualized way to evaluate performance, set realistic targets, and understand the scale of development challenges.
goal_progress.config.citation: Mahler, D G and Lambrechts, M. 2026. “Measuring Progress” In Atlas of Sustainable Development Goals 2026,  edited by A. F. Pirlea, D. Wadhwa, D. Mahler, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/pp3f-d004
goal_progress.config.inputs: Daniel Boller, Bob Rijkers, Nishant Yonzan
goal_progress.content.#1.text: Tracking progress toward development goals is crucial for countries to identify areas to prioritize and learn from their own—and other countries’— successes and failures.
goal_progress.content.#2.text: India’s open defecation challenge is a good example[emphasis: . ]Lagging behind its peers and its own target for ending open defecation, in 2014 the government launched the Swachh Bharat Mission to build toilets and change behavior regarding sanitation practices. During the first five years (Phase 1), the mission built over 100 million toilets and the proportion of the population practicing open defecation fell from 33 percent to 20 percent—a 40 percent drop.[reference: unsdg] Progress has since continued, and in 2024, only around 7 percent of the population practiced open defecation.
goal_progress.content.#3.text: Cases like this illustrate how understanding a country's progress, or lack of it, can drive policy change and document achievements. Although India’s achievements are impressive, how can we quantify how well it did over this time span? It is not as trivial as it may sound.
goal_progress.content.#4.text: When measuring progress toward a development indicator, a common first step is to track whether a country has met a specific target, such as a Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) target. But tracking in this way overlooks that, despite making significant progress, a country can still fall short of a target.[reference: easterly2009][reference: clemens2007] In this case, despite the massive headways made by the Swachh Bharat Mission, India’s 7 percent open defecation rate of 2024 is above SDG target 6.2, which calls for an end to open defecation.
goal_progress.content.#5.text: Another way of measuring progress is to compare across countries the changes they have experienced. In this case, we could compare the decline in the proportion of people practicing open defecation over five years using percent (which for India was 40 percent) or percentage points (13 percentage points, from 33 percent to 20 percent).
goal_progress.content.#6.text: These two simple and frequently used measures of progress can paint very different pictures. To see this difference, let us take a look at Côte d’Ivoire and Georgia, and their recent progress in reducing extreme poverty.
goal_progress.content.#7.title: Which country made the most progress?
goal_progress.content.#7.subtitle: Poverty rate (%)
goal_progress.content.#7.source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform, World Bank Macro and Poverty Outlook April 2026.
goal_progress.content.#7.visdescription: Compares progress in poverty reduction in Côte d’Ivoire and Georgia from 2015 to 2025 using different common metrics of progress.
goal_progress.content.#7.scenes.#0.text: Between 2015 and 2025, both countries made impressive reductions in poverty, with Côte d’Ivoire reducing poverty from 26.3 percent to 16.1 percent and Georgia from 10.6 percent to 3.5 percent.
goal_progress.content.#7.scenes.#1.text: If we compare this in [emphasis: percentage points], Côte d’Ivoire has made the most progress, with 10.2 percent of its population escaping poverty over the 10-year period, compared to around 7.1 percent of Georgia’s population.
goal_progress.content.#7.scenes.#2.text: Yet Georgia could hardly have matched Côte d’Ivoire’s feat, as only 10.6 percent of the population was in poverty at the start of the period. If we look instead at the decline in [emphasis: percent] (reflecting the share of the poor population that has escaped poverty), then Georgia made the most progress, as 67 percent of its poor population escaped poverty, compared to 39 percent in Côte d’Ivoire.
goal_progress.content.#9.text: It is unclear which country has made more progress because standard measures provide conflicting answers. This is because these common measures inherently favor or disadvantage specific stages of development.[reference: klasen2012] To accurately gauge a country's progress, we need to account for the fact that large changes are more likely during certain phases of development than others.[reference: allwine2016][reference: ravallion2012] Let us take a look now at how we can apply this for extreme poverty rates.
goal_progress.content.#10.title: The relationship between changes in poverty and levels of poverty
goal_progress.content.#10.source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform, World Bank Macro and Poverty Outlook April 2026, Mahler et al. (2025a), and Mahler et al. (2025b).
goal_progress.content.#10.visdescription: Shows the typical changes in poverty rates as a function of initial changes in poverty rates, and how this predicts typical trajectories of poverty rates.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#0.text: First, we use all poverty data around the world since 1950 to plot changes in poverty rates over five years as a function of the poverty rate at the start of the five-year period (baseline poverty rate).
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#1.text: Next, we estimate the typical change in poverty as a function of the level of poverty. That is, we divide the observed changes so that at every baseline poverty rate, approximately half are above the line and half are below it.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#2.text: Changes in poverty are typically smallest (in absolute terms) for very low and very high poverty rates and largest for poverty rates around 50 percent.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#3.text: A country with a 50 percent poverty rate typically decreases poverty by 0.73 percentage points the following year…
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#4.text: …while a country with a 5 percent poverty rate typically decreases poverty by 0.22 percentage points the following year.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#5.text: From this, we can trace out a typical trajectory from any starting point. If we look at Côte d’Ivoire’s 2015 poverty rate of 26.3 percent, the typical change is -0.65.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#6.text: So, if Côte d’Ivoire had followed the typical pattern, in 2016, its poverty rate would have been 25.6 percent.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#7.text: Following this method iteratively, we can trace the typical path a country with Côte d’Ivoire’s starting point would have followed over 10 years.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#8.text: Next, we compare that with Côte d’Ivoire’s actual trend from 2015 to 2025.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#9.text: The progress Côte d’Ivoire achieved between 2015 and 2025 would have typically taken until 2033. In other words, Côte d’Ivoire achieved in 10 years what would typically take 17 years. We denote this a speed of 1.7, reflecting that Côte d’Ivoire developed nearly twice as fast as the typical experience.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#10.tex: If we do the same for Georgia, we can see that its progress over 10 years was equivalent to what typically takes 25 years, giving us a speed of 2.5 suggesting that Georgia made faster progress than Côte d’Ivoire.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#11.tex: Let us now take a look at the pathway for a country that starts entrenched in extreme poverty.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#12.tex: At the typical speed of progress, ending poverty would take two and a half centuries.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#13.tex: Let us now compare how far countries moved on this trajectory from 2015 and 2025.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#14.tex: The black circle denotes Côte d'Ivoire’s position in 2015.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#15.tex: Over the next 10 years, Côte d'Ivoire reduced poverty by 10.2 percentage points, moving almost twice as fast as the typical rate of countries at a similar level of development.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#16.tex: Over the same 10 years, Georgia reduced its poverty rate by 7 percentage points, a reduction typically seen over a period of 25 years. So Georgia moved at a speed of three.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#17.tex: Sierra Leone, by contrast, made no progress and it moved at a speed of zero.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#18.tex: In Malawi, progress reversed, and it moved at a speed of minus one, undoing about one year of typical progress every year.
goal_progress.content.#10.scenes.#19.tex: If we add all countries, we can see that those that made large positive headways on the path had a high speed of progress, and vice versa.
goal_progress.content.#11.text: The future and setting targets beyond 2030
goal_progress.content.#12.text: At the typical speed of development, going from very high poverty rates to a society free of extreme poverty can take centuries. This also applies to other domains of developmentl.[reference: mahler2025a] Even if countries move drastically faster than the typical speed of development—like Singapore and the Republic of Korea have done since the 1950s—transitioning from the worst-observed outcomes to some of the best easily takes 75 years.
goal_progress.content.#13.text: The long road to development suggests that some SDG targets—such as complete access to services or elimination of deprivations—were plausible for some countries but virtually impossible for others, which are generations away from reaching these levels. Globally ambitious targets are harder to achieve for poorer countries, which need much faster speeds of progress to obtain the same level as countries starting from better positions. Yet perceived success and failure has been closely associated with the outcome of these targets.[reference: waage2010]
goal_progress.content.#14.text: So, how should countries set realistic and aspirational targets? We can use the speed scores explained above to create targets at varying levels of ambition and with various comparator countries.
goal_progress.content.#15.title: Possible poverty targets for Côte d'Ivoire
goal_progress.content.#15.rate2: Continuing its own speed from 2015–25 (2x typical speed)
goal_progress.content.#15.source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform, World Bank Macro and Poverty Outlook October 2025, Mahler et al. (2025a), and Mahler et al. (2025b).
goal_progress.content.#15.visdescription: The visualization shows poverty rates Côte d'Ivoire could target by 2050.
goal_progress.content.#15.scenes.#0.text: In 2025, Côte d’Ivoire’s poverty rate was 15.9 percent.
goal_progress.content.#15.scenes.#1.text: At the typical speed of progress, poverty will be driven down to 6.8 percent in 2050. This is one possible 2050 target for Côte d’Ivoire.
goal_progress.content.#15.scenes.#2.text: But Côte d’Ivoire moved twice as fast as the typical speed in the past decade. If it continues at this speed, it would reach a poverty rate of 2.6 percent by 2050.
goal_progress.content.#15.scenes.#3.text: It could also choose an even more ambitious target, such as moving at four times the typical speed. This would reflect some of the fastest movers we have seen. Under such a scenario, it should target a 0.5 percent poverty rate by 2050.
goal_progress.content.#16.text: Countries should not necessarily consider such high speeds of development for their targets. Those with less capacity to develop—for example, due to high debt levels or conflict—may prefer to choose a target based on their own, slower historical pace of progress. But others could choose to define their target by following the speed of an aspirational set of countries—say, the best performers in their region.
goal_progress.content.#18.text: The method presented here and most other measures of progress face limitations that are important to consider.
goal_progress.content.#19.text: [emphasis: First, this method cannot be applied to all indicators. ]The speed method does not work, for example, for indicators where countries on expectation have not made historical progress, such as forest cover. For such cases, we have developed a complementary way of measuring progress that gives all countries a score from 0–100 that reflects the share of historical experiences they are outperforming.[reference: mahler2025a] For example, a score of 80 means that a country is progressing faster than 80 percent of all the cases observed historically.
goal_progress.content.#20.text: [emphasis: Second, measuring progress is difficult when indicators change drastically from year to year, such as when measuring deaths from natural disasters]. Even if a country is making fast progress on its emergency preparedness and adaptation to climate change, one rare natural disaster could suggest a lack of progress.
goal_progress.content.#21.text: [emphasis: Third, the speed method requires indicators with a large amount of data to estimate expected changes with enough accuracy. ]As a result, indicators that lack data over a long time period or a large set of countries will involve greater uncertainty.
goal_progress.content.#22.text: [emphasis: Finally, the speed method is limited because we do not observe all countries at all stages of development. ]For example, to estimate how countries typically progress at high levels of development, we can only rely on information from countries that have achieved such standards. Conversely, we do not have data at low levels of development for most of the countries that are currently highly developed.
goal_progress.references.easterly2009: Easterly, W. 2009. "How the Millennium Development Goals are Unfair to Africa." World Development 37(1): 26–35.
goal_progress.references.mahler2025a: Mahler, D G, Serajuddin, U, Wadhwa, D and Yonzan, N. 2025. “The World Is Developing at Its Slowest Pace in 75 Years.”
goal_progress.references.mahler2025b: Mahler, D G, Foster, E, Lakner, C, Prinsloo, Z, Tchouakam Mbouendeu, R and Tetteh-Baah, S K. 2025. Constructing Comparable Global Poverty Trends. World Bank.
goal_progress.references.worldbank2025: World Bank. 2025. Macro Poverty Outlook October 2025.
goal_progress.references.waage2010: Waage, J, Banerji, R, Campbell, O, Chirwa, E, Collender, G, Dieltiens, V, Dorward, A, Godfrey-Faussett, P, Hanvoravongchai, P, Kingdon, G et al. 2010. "The Millennium Development Goals: A Cross-sectoral Analysis and Principles for Goal Setting after 2015: Lancet and London International Development Centre Commission." The Lancet 376(9745): 991–1023.
goal_progress.references.swachh: Department of Drinking Water & Sanitation . “About Swachh Bharat Mission.” Ministry of Jal Shakti, Government of India. Accessed October 15, 2025.
goal_progress.references.unsdg: United Nations Statistics Division, “Global SDG Indicators Platform.” Accessed October 17, 2025.
goal_progress.references.allwine2016: Allwine, M, Rigolini, J and López-Calva, L F. 2016. "The Unfairness of (Poverty) Targets." Oxford Economic Papers 68(2): 379–397.
goal_progress.references.clemens2007: Clemens, M A, Kenny, C J and Moss, T J. 2007. "The Trouble with the MDGs: Confronting Expectations of Aid and Development Success." World Development 35(5): 735–751.
goal_progress.references.ravallion2012: Ravallion, Martin. 2012. "Why Don't We See Poverty Convergence?" American Economic Review 102(1): 504–523.
goal_progress.references.klasen2012: Klasen S and Lange, S. 2012. “Getting Progress Right: Measuring Progress Towards the MDGs Against Historical Trends.” FERDi.
